coredata.dc:title: Perylenetetracarboxylic acid and carbon quantum dots assembled synergistic electrochemiluminescence nanomaterial for ultra-sensitive carcinoembryonic antigen detection
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Lan-lan
Zhang, Wei
Shang, Lei
Ma, Rong-na
Jia, Li-ping
Jia, Wen-li
Wang, Huai-sheng
Niu, Li
coredata.dc:description: It is important to design a nice electrochemiluminescence (ECL) biological nanomaterial for fabricating sensitive ECL immunosensor to detect tumor markers. Most reported ECL nanomaterial was decorated by a number of mono-luminophore. Here, we report a novel ECL nanomaterial assembled by dual luminophores perylenetetracarboxylic acid (PTCA) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs). In the ECL nanomaterial, graphene was chosen as nanocarrier. Significant ECL intensity increases are seen in the ECL nanomaterial, which was interpreted with the proposed synergistic promotion ECL meachanism of PTCA and CQDs. Furthermore, this ECL nanomaterial was used to label secondary antibody and fabricate a sandwiched carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) immunosensor. The CEA immunosensor exhibits high sensitivity and the linear semilogarithmical range was from 0.001fgmL−1 to 1ngmL−1 with low detection limit 0.00026fgmL−1. And the CEA immunosensor is also suitable for various cancers’ sample detection providing potential specific applications in diagnostics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Perylenetetracarboxylic acid
Carbon quantum dots
Synergistic electrochemiluminescence
Carcinoembryonic antigen detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of CQDs and AuNPs
Immobilization of Ab2 on the graphene-PTCA-Cu2+-CQDs/AuNPs nanomaterial
Fabrication of the ECL sandwiched immunosensor
Characterization of PTCA and CQDs assembled nanomaterial
ECL mechanism of PTCA and CQDs assembled nanomaterial
Fundamental electrochemical properties of constructed sandwiched ECL immunosensor
Reproducibility, stability and selectivity of the immunosensor
Application of the immunosensor in human serum samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) the UV–vis absorbance spectroscopy of PTCA(a), graphene-PTCA(b), GO(c) and graphene(d); (B) The FT-IR transmission spectroscopy of PTCA (a), graphene-PTCA(b) and GO(c). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The TEM images of GO(A); graphene-PTCA(B); graphene-PTCA-Cu2+-CQDs(C) and graphene-PTCA-Cu2+-CQDs/AuNPs/Ab2(D). Inset is the corresponding schematic illustration.

(A) the ECL of graphene, PTCA, graphene-PTCA, graphene-PTCA-CQDs, graphene-PTCA-Cu2+, graphene-PTCA-Cu2+-CQDs modified GCE in 0.1M PBS (pH7.4) with 0.1M K2S2O8 at the scanning potential range from − 0.2V to − 1.8V. (B) the electrochemistry impedance spectroscopy of bare GCE(a), GCE/Au(b), GCE/Au/Ab1(c), GCE/Au/Ab1/BSA (d), GCE/Au/Ab1/CEA (e); GCE/Au/Ab1/CEA/Ab2/AuNPs/CQDs-Cu2+-PTCA-graphene(f).

(A) the ECL detection of CEA at different concentration from 0.001fgmL−1(a) to 1ngmL−1(f); (B) A plot of ΔECL vs. the logarithm of CEA concentrations. (C) Continuous cyclic potential scans of the proposed immunosensor at CCEA 0.01pgmL−1; (D) Specificity of the ECL immunosensor toward (a) blank control, (b) 0.51mgmL−1 Cholesterol, (c) 0.6mgmL−1 Uric Acid, (d) 20mgmL−1 Glucose, (e) 600mgmL−1 HSA, (f) 1ngmL−1 CEA.
(a) The layer by layer assemble process of graphene-PTCA-Cu2+-CQDs/AuNPs/Ab2; (b) the fabricated process of proposed ECL immunosensor and the synergistic ECL principle.

head.ce:title: Perylenetetracarboxylic acid and carbon quantum dots assembled synergistic electrochemiluminescence nanomaterial for ultra-sensitive carcinoembryonic antigen detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng, Shandong, 252059, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, c/o Engineering Laboratory for Modern Analytical Techniques, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Renmin St.5625, Changchun 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, c/o Engineering Laboratory for Modern Analytical Techniques, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is important to design a nice electrochemiluminescence (ECL) biological nanomaterial for fabricating sensitive ECL immunosensor to detect tumor markers. Most reported ECL nanomaterial was decorated by a number of mono-luminophore. Here, we report a novel ECL nanomaterial assembled by dual luminophores perylenetetracarboxylic acid (PTCA) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs). In the ECL nanomaterial, graphene was chosen as nanocarrier. Significant ECL intensity increases are seen in the ECL nanomaterial, which was interpreted with the proposed synergistic promotion ECL meachanism of PTCA and CQDs. Furthermore, this ECL nanomaterial was used to label secondary antibody and fabricate a sandwiched carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) immunosensor. The CEA immunosensor exhibits high sensitivity and the linear semilogarithmical range was from 0.001fgmL−1 to 1ngmL−1 with low detection limit 0.00026fgmL−1. And the CEA immunosensor is also suitable for various cancers’ sample detection providing potential specific applications in diagnostics.

ce:section.ce:para: Many tumor markers, for example DNA methylation markers (Hao et al., 2017), carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) (Hasanzadeh et al., 2017b), have been screened and exhibit high specificity for certain cancers, including prostate, liver, lung, and colorectal cancer (Tang and Ma, 2017; Xiang et al., 2017). The slight change of its concentration is tightly related to the occurrence, development and palindromia of cancer, and the therapeutic effect (Hasanzadeh et al., 2017a). Thus, the development of effective methods to sensitively detect the slight change of concentration of tumor markers was urgent for clinical diagnosis (Song et al., 2017; Tawa et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2017). A number of immunoassay methods, such as fluoroimmunoassay (Ramezani and Jed Harrison, 2015), colorimetric immunoassay (Zhang et al., 2017; Li et al., 2017a), dynamic light scattering immunoassay (Li et al., 2016) and electrochemical immunoassay (Cai et al., 2016) have been developed.

Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) immunoassay has gained increasing attention due to the advantages such as high sensitivity, good selectivity, low background signal and rapid response (Liu et al., 2015). A series of tumor markers was ultra-sensitively detected by fabricated sandwiched ECL immunosensors (primary antibody-antigen-secondary antibody (Liu et al., 2017c; Zhu et al., 2018). In this case, the secondary antibody was often labeled with mono-luminophore decorated nanomaterial (Jiang et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2016). The sensor's sensitivity depends on the luminophore's amount and luminous efficiency on the nanomaterial. Thus, a few characteristic features of luminophore and nanocarrier were required to design a nice ECL nanomaterial. It needs stable and high luminous efficiency and various active functional groups for luminophore. Large surface area and good electron conductivity is essential for nanocarrier such as Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) (Shuai et al., 2017a; Liu et al., 2017b), carbon nanotubes (Thapa et al., 2017) and graphene (Justino et al., 2017; Juang et al., 2017). Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were widely used in ECL biosensor (Liu et al., 2017a), bioimaging (Wu et al., 2016), nanomedicine (Peng et al., 2017) and photocatalysis (Wang et al., 2017b)fields, because of its rich functional groups such as carboxyl group and hydroxyl group, as well as low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. To fabricate one composited nanomaterial assembled by multiple luminophores with synergistic promotion effect in ECL gives us a new perspective on designing biological nanomaterial. An organic molecule with large π-conjugated structure, perylenetetracarboxylic acid (PTCA), can produce strong ECL signal with S2O4 2- as coreactant at the same potential range from − 0.2V to − 1.8V as CQDs (Lei et al., 2016).

In this work, we exploit PTCA and CQDs as dual luminophores and graphene as nanocarrier to fabricate ECL nanomaterial. In this nanomaterial, PTCA can non-covalently modify graphene (Hu et al., 2011), meanwhile, be used as a bridge to highly load CQDs through Cu2+. PTCA and CQDs exhibits synergistic ECL effect with S2O4 2- as coreactant. AuNPs were also grafted onto the nanomaterial for immobilizing secondary antibody. CEA analysis was taken as an example to prove a biological application. The detection principle is shown in Scheme 1. We immobilize primary antibody(Ab1) on the Au film modified GCE. And the complex of graphene-PTCA-Cu2+-CQDs/AuNPs was used to immobilize secondary antibody(Ab2). The results demonstrated the graphene-PTCA-Cu2+-CQDs/AuNPs nanomaterial was highly sensitive for CEA detection and specific recognition.

Human colon cancer carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) and carcinoembryonic antigen antibody (anti-CEA) were purchased from Linc-Bio Science Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). HAuCl4·4H2O, bovineserum albumin (BSA), perylene-3, 4, 9, 10-tetracarboxylic dianhydride (PTCDA, 97%) were obtained from Sigma–Aldrich. Citric acid, ethylenediamine, and uric acid (UA) were purchased from Aladdin Industry Corporation. Human serum samples were provided by Liaocheng People's Hospital. All chemical reagents were analytical grade and used as received. All aqueous solutions were prepared with ultra-pure water (> 18MΩ) from a Milli-Q Plus system (Millipore).

Electrochemiluminescence measurements were carried out on a Model RFL-1 ECL analyzer (Xi’an Remex Instrument Co., Ltd., China) with the voltage of the photomultiplier tube (PMT) set at 800V and with the auxiliary equipment of CHI 990B (Shanghai CHI Instruments Co., China) electrochemical workstation. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed using a CHI 760C electrochemical workstation. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained on a JEM-2100 electron microscope operating at 200kV (JEOL Ltd., Japan). UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained using Lambda 750 spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was recorded using a Bruker Tensor 27 Spectrometer.

PTCA was prepared according to a reported literature (Gan et al., 2016). In brief, 1g PTCDA and 25mL of 1molL−1 NaOH were added into the beaker and stirred for half one hour, then washed several times by ultra-water. The obtained yellow product was dried at 60℃. Graphene oxide (GO) was prepared according to Hummer's method. Graphene-PTCA was synthesized according to a previous method. Briefly, 50mL of 2mgmL−1 GO, 150mg PTCA, 25μL of 5% N2H4 and 0.5mL of 25% NH3·H2O were added into the one-neck flask and refluxed with magnetic stirring under 95℃ for 40min. The mixture solution was transferred into the MWCO 14,000 dialysis membrane to dialyze by ultra-pure water for four days. The obtained pale violet red color solution was graphene-PTCA and it was stored at 4°C for further characterization and use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CQDs and AuNPs
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs and AuNPs were prepared according to a reported method with a little modification (Li et al., 2017b). The detail can be found in Supporting information(SI).

400μL of 0.1mgmL−1 graphene-PTCA and 20μL of 2molL−1 CuCl2 were added into the beaker and stirred at room temperature for 30min, then 2mL of 0.1mgmL−1 CQDs and 200μL AuNPs were added and stirred for another one hour. The resulting product graphene-PTCA-Cu2+-CQDs/AuNPs was centrifuged and dispersed in PBS (pH 7.4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immobilization of Ab2 on the graphene-PTCA-Cu2+-CQDs/AuNPs nanomaterial
ce:section.ce:para: 20μL of 1.25mgmL−1 Ab2 was added into graphene-PTCA-Cu2+-CQDs/AuNPs solution and stirred for 30min. Then 20μL of 5% BSA was added into the above solution to block the residual active sites at 4°C. The resultant solution was centrifuged at 10,000rpm for three times, then dispersed in 1mL PBS (pH 7.4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the ECL sandwiched immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication process of the ECL sandwiched immunosensor was shown in Scheme 1. Firstly, the chronoamperometric mode was used for the electrodeposition of AuNPs on the GCE. The electrodeposition was applied for a potential of −0.2V (vs. Ag/AgCl) for 200s to the GCE immersed into a 8mM HAuCl4. Then 5μL of 18μgmL−1 anti-CEA (Ab1) solution was dropped onto the GCE/Au film surface and the electrode was incubated at 4°C for 12h to get GCE/Au/Ab1. Subsequently, 5μL of 5% BSA solution was dropped on the GCE/Au/Ab1 and incubated at 37°C for 1h. Next, the GCE/Au/Ab1 electrode was incubated in different concentrations of CEA solution at 37°C for 1h. Finally, 5μL graphene-PTCA-Cu2+-CQDs/AuNPs/Ab2 solution was dropped on the GCE/Au/Ab1/CEA for immunoreaction at 37°C for 1h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of PTCA and CQDs assembled nanomaterial
ce:section.ce:para: PTCA comprises a five-connected benzene ring and four carboxyl groups. It dispersed well in water and form a yellow-green solution ( Fig. 1A, a). PTCA is a good ECL luminophore, which produce strong and stable ECL signal as the same as CQDs with S2O8 2- as coreactant (Fig. S5A, S3A). And the ECL intensity of PTCA was relative to the applied potential. With the potential became larger, the ECL intensity exponentially increased(Fig. S5B). The ECL mechanism maybe that the coreactant of S2O8 2- reacted with reduced species of PTCA (PTCA-·) resulting in production of the excited state of PTCA (PTCA*), which emitted light (Lei et al., 2016).

Graphene features a large flat surface area, high electron conductivity and good electron acceptor, which make it an excellent candidate in extensive applications such as sensors (Shuai et al., 2017b; Chen et al., 2017) drug delivery and disease diagnosis. For example, the large flat surface area can load large quantities of biomolecules (Chen et al., 2018), drugs and nanoparticles (Shuai et al., 2017c). Another typical characteristic of the π-conjugated structure is that it could act as a non-covalent functionalization platform (π–π interaction). In this study, graphene was chosen as the nanocarrier of PTCA and CQDs dual luminophores assembled nanomaterial.

The graphene-PTCA hybrid was prepared by refluxing the mixture of GO and PTCA at 95°C. In the process, GO completely reduced to graphene, which can be inferred from the control experiment. In the control experiment, the yellow GO solution changed to aggregated dark brown nanosheets(Fig. 1A, inset c and d), which should be due to the loss of epoxy, hydroxyl group in the reduction process (Zhang et al., 2014). And the UV–vis absorption peak changed from 228nm to 267nm due to the recovered π−π system(Fig. 1A, c and d). The Raman spectrum of graphene was also shown in Fig. S1. And the π−π interaction between the sp2 plane of graphene and five-benzene core of PTCA drived PTCA to self-assemble on graphene. The got graphene-PTCA hybrid is more hydrophilic than graphene(Fig. 1A, inset b and d) due to the solvophilic carboxylic groups of PTCA. The three UV–vis absorption peaks of PTCA around 413, 437 and 467nm remarkably red shifted after self-assembly on graphene, indicating the aggregation of PTCA on graphene(Fig. 1A, b). The IR spectra reserved finger-print information of PTCA between 1000cm−1 and 2000cm−1 (Fig. 1B, b). A dense layer of “nanobud-like” supramolecular nanostructures on graphene was observed on the TEM image( Fig. 2B), which was consistent with the UV–vis results and indicated PTCA stacked in a face-to-face mode on graphene as reported by Niu's group (Gan et al., 2016). In addition, the high density of PTCA on graphene indicated the potential to further highly load CQDs. The characterization of prepared CQDs demonstrates the CQDs were uniform with diameter 3–4nm (Fig. S3A). On the surface of CQDs, there are lots of -OH and -COOH groups according to the IR spectrum of CQDs (Fig. S2B).

CQDs were anchored onto graphene-PTCA nanosheet through Cu2+, which has strong coordination interaction with -COOH group of the three nanomaterials (Wang et al., 2017a). We can obviously noted CQDs loading on the graphene-PTCA-Cu2+ with high density from the TEM (Fig. 2C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL mechanism of PTCA and CQDs assembled nanomaterial
ce:section.ce:para: The ECL property of the dual luminophores PTCA and CQDs hybrided nanomaterial was investigated ( Fig. 3A). PTCA modified GCE produce strong ECL. After PTCA self-assembled on the graphene, the ECL didn’t increase due to no ECL contribution of graphene (Fig. 3A). When Cu2+ adsorped onto graphene-PTCA surface, the ECL increased a little. Further CQDs were anchored onto graphene-PTCA-Cu2+ surface, the ECL increased largely, versus, in the absence of Cu2+, no ECL increase for graphene-PTCA-CQDs. The results show that CQDs successfully highly loaded on the graphene-PTCA in the presence of Cu2+, which was consistent with the aforementioned TEM result.

And the result also demonstrated PTCA and CQDs synergistically promoted the ECL. Take consideration of Yuan's (Lei et al., 2016) and our group's result (Li et al., 2017b), the ECL mechanism maybe the coreactant of S2O8 2- reacted with reduced species of PTCA (PTCA-·) and CQDs (CQDs-·) resulting in production of the excited state of PTCA (PTCA*) and CQDs (CQDs*), both of which emitted light, respectively. And it was illustrated as follows:
(1) CQDs + e- → CQDs-·
(2) PTCA + e- → PTCA-·
(3)

CQDs

CQDs * + SO
4

(5)
PTCA
− •

4

PTCA * + SO

(6)
CQDs * → CQDs + h v
(7)
PTCA * → PTCA + h v

Then AuNPs around 13nm (Fig. S3B), reduced by citrate, were anchored onto the nice ECL nanomaterial (Fig. S3C). The prepared graphene-PTCA-Cu2+-CQDs/AuNPs nanomaterial finally were used to label Ab2. Then BSA was used to seal the non-active surface of the AuNPs to avoid non-specific adsorption. And Fig. 2D shows the flower-like nanostructure of AuNPs on the nanohybrid after the introduction of Ab2 and BSA. And the graphene-PTCA-Cu2+-CQDs/AuNPs/Ab2 nanomaterial modified GCE also produce large ECL (Fig. S4B), which indicated the combined nanomaterial was excellent ECL nanomaterial, favorable for preparing ultra-sensitive immunosensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fundamental electrochemical properties of constructed sandwiched ECL immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: We exploited AuNPs film modified GCE as platform to immobilize Ab1. Then GCE/Au/Ab1 was used to capture CEA and Ab2 labeled by nanohybrid for constructing sandwiched ECL immunosensor. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used to investigate the construction process of ECL immunosensor by using [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- couple as the electrochemical redox probe. The electrodeposited AuNPs film is high conductive and can accelerate the electron transfer showing a smaller electron transfer resistance (R ct) than bare GCE (Fig. 3B, b). The electrodeposited AuNPs film has plentiful active site for immobilizing amount of Ab1, which set up good platform for constructing sensitive sandwiched ECL immunosensor. When Ab1 adsorped onto the AuNPs film surface, the formed Ab1 layer is not good electrical conductor, and slows down the electron transfer from electrode to solution (Fig. 3B, c). BSA was used to seal the in-active site on the AuNPs film. As same with Ab1, R ct becames larger and larger(Fig. 3B, d,e) with the stepwised introduction of BSA and CEA. Finally, graphene-PTCA-Cu2+-CQDs/AuNPs/Ab2 nanomaterial was introduced onto the electrode to construct the complete CEA ECL immunosensor. The R ct decreased dramatically(Fig. 3 B, f), which was due to the high conductivity of graphene-PTCA-Cu2+-CQDs/AuNPs nanohybrid.

The ECL experiment was carried out to check the constructed sandwiched immunosensor. With the increase of CEA concentration, the ECL intensity stepwisely increased until CEA concentration be up to 1ngmL−1( Fig. 4A). And the difference (ΔECL) in the ECL intensity before and after CEA immobilization was adopted as the measurement signal. The ΔECL value was linearly with the logarithm value of CEA concentration over the range from 0.001fgmL−1 to 1ngmL−1. The regression equation was ΔECL = 6428.3 + 935.7 lgCCEA/pgmL−1 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9986 (Fig. 4B). The detection limit for CEA was calculated to be 0.00026fgmL−1 with 3σ (derived from the criterion of IUPAC recommendation. In this equation, σ was the standard deviation of the 11 parallel measurements of blank solution). The result indicated that the proposed ECL sandwiched immunosensor was prospected to sensitively detect CEA.

In addition, compared with other ECL immunosensor based on mono-luminophore decorated nanomaterial shown in Table S1, the proposed immunosensor has higher efficiency with lower detection limitation and relatively wide detection range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility, stability and selectivity of the immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The reproducibility and stability were two major factors for practical application of immunosensors. By preparing four parallel electrodes under the same condition, the reproducibility of the proposed immunosensor was investigated. At the CEA concentration of 1pgmL−1, the four immunosensors’ ECL intensity was 9536, 9212, 9044, 9798 respectively. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of ECL intensity of the four immunosensors was 3.58%. The ECL intensity of the proposed immunosensor under continuously cyclic potential scanning for 10 cycles was almost the same, which indicated the good stability at ECL experiment(Fig. 4C). In a word, the proposed immunosensor had good reproducibility and stability.

The specificity of the immunosensor had an important effect on the analysis of biological samples. Therefore, some substances that exist in human blood such as human serum albumin, cholesterol, glucose and uric acid were used as the interference to assess the specificity. 0.51mgmL−1 cholesterol, 20mgmL−1 of glucose, 600mgmL−1 human serum albumin(HSA), 0.6mgmL−1 uric acid and 1ngmL−1 of CEA were detected by the proposed immunosensor, respectively (Fig. 4D). The ECL intensity of different interferences was far below the target CEA, which indicated the prepared immunosensor had high selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the immunosensor in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: Human serum samples from different cancer people were diluted for one hundred times and used to monitor the feasibility of the proposed ECL immunosensor. The concentrations of CEA in these diluted samples obtained by the proposed ECL immunosensor and those obtained by ELISA were listed in Table S2. The results indicated the relative errors were at an acceptable and satisfactory numerical range. Therefore, the proposed ECL sandwiched immunosensor provided a valuable tool for detecting CEA in real samples.

In summary, we prepared a novel ECL nanomaterial assembled by PTCA and CQDs as dual luminophores. Significant ECL intensity increases are seen in this nanomaterial, which is interpreted as the synergistic promotion meachanism of PTCA and CQDs. The prepared CEA ECL immunosensor based on the ECL nanomaterial displays a sensitive ECL response and is also suitable for detecting human serum sample from various cancers. This work provides a new perspective for designing ECL biological nanomaterial applicated in tumor markers’ sensitive detection for clinical diagnosis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2017BB084 and ZR2015BQ007) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (21505063, 21405070, 21375055 and 21427808).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.10.035.
